label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, so I need to figure out which of the two statements is sarcastic. Let's start by looking at the options provided. Option A says, "One of the worst things to do working in retail was pushing a trolley of returns around the store putting them back." The key words here are "worst things to do." The person is clearly stating that pushing the trolley of returns is a bad or unpleasant task. This sounds straightforward and not sarcastic. They're simply expressing that this is a negative part of their job. Option B states, "One of the enjoyable things to do working in retail was pushing a trolley of returns around the store putting them back." Here, the key phrase is "enjoyable things to do." Now, pushing a trolley of returns is generally not something people would consider enjoyable. It's usually seen as a tedious or boring task. So, when someone says that this was an "enjoyable" part of their job, they might be using sarcasm to indicate the opposite—that it was actually not enjoyable at all. This kind of contrast between what is said and what is likely meant is a common feature of sarcasm. Let me think further. Sarcasm often involves saying the opposite of what is meant in a way that is clearly ironic or mocking. In retail work, pushing a trolley of returns is typically not a fun or enjoyable activity; it's repetitive and can be seen as a chore. If someone were to call it an "enjoyable" task, they might be being sarcastic to highlight how unenjoyable it actually was. Another angle to consider is the context in which these statements might be made. If someone is complaining about their job in retail, they might use sarcasm to emphasize their frustration or boredom.
